subject: David Gergen
disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY WITH HONOR ACTION, INC
body:
| | | | | [View this email in your browser](https://mailchi.mp/51d33febc50d/invitation-two-parties-two-veterans-one-conversation-rep-lalota-rep-ryan-special-session-with-president-carter-alumni-14053377?e=33f53a5b1a)
---
| | | | [ ](https://withhonor.us16.list-manage.com/track/click?u=80940e6d1b1e08928d721cf8d&id=343137604f&e=33f53a5b1a)
---
| | | | Friends,

David Gergen’s son, Christopher, called With Honor Co-Founder and CEO Rye Barcott this morning to share that his father, our co-founder David Gergen, had passed away peacefully. [This](https://withhonor.us16.list-manage.com/track/click?u=80940e6d1b1e08928d721cf8d&id=a4050cd380&e=33f53a5b1a) is a link to the Washington Post obituary that just posted. Below is a tribute Rye wrote after one of his final visits with David.

If you would care to contribute something in David's honor, please consider giving to the [Gergen Public Service Fellowships at Harvard’s Center for Public Leadership](https://withhonor.us16.list-manage.com/track/click?u=80940e6d1b1e08928d721cf8d&id=e8f395049e&e=33f53a5b1a) via [this link](https://withhonor.us16.list-manage.com/track/click?u=80940e6d1b1e08928d721cf8d&id=4e003d8613&e=33f53a5b1a). David’s former students created the fellowship on his 80th birthday a few years ago, and the second class of summer fellows is currently serving across the country.

We will host a special tribute to David later this year at our biennial gala for the veterans we support in the 119th Congress. You can listen to his closing remarks from our 118th Gala at [this link](https://withhonor.us16.list-manage.com/track/click?u=80940e6d1b1e08928d721cf8d&id=bc1419ac02&e=33f53a5b1a).
---
|
---
| [**Harvard Center for Public Leadership: Rye Barcott reflects on five pieces of advice from David Gergen**](https://withhonor.us16.list-manage.com/track/click?u=80940e6d1b1e08928d721cf8d&id=4e5c5805be&e=33f53a5b1a)

I’m among the lucky ones to have had David Gergen as a teacher. When I graduated from Harvard Kennedy School in 2009, he became a mentor. Years later, when I started a nonprofit, he became a cofounder. Now, he is a cherished friend.

An action-oriented person, I think David would most appreciate my sharing in a tribute to him advice that I learned from him that can be useful to others pursuing lives of service. Here are five pieces of advice I gained from the great good fortune of knowing David Gergen.

**Lesson one: use lists**

I might as well start with this one, perhaps the most pragmatic pieces of advice from David.

Months after completing my third overseas tour as a Marine in Fallujah, Iraq, I joined the Kennedy School on a fellowship David had established with veterans in mind at Harvard's Center for Public Leadership (CPL). I knew I wanted to study under David. He was one of the few balanced voices of reason on cable news, and I had watched television anchors and commentators with keen interest ever since replying to a second-grade survey on what I wanted to be when I grew up with, "Tom Brokaw."

Unfortunately, David’s class was full my first semester. We had met through a mutual friend in North Carolina, and I had a more of a personal relationship with him from the outset because we shared ties to the Old North State, where he grew up, and where I earned my bachelor's degree and intended to settle after graduation with my amazing [University of North Carolina Chapel Hill] Tar Heel wife. So, I approached him one day and proposed an independent study on speechwriting, which he had a singular point of view on, having written and edited speeches for four sitting U.S. presidents. While David had not offered an independent study focused on speechwriting before, he was interested in new ideas, appreciated my initiative, and wanted to help me with my career development.

Of course, his time was stretched well beyond that of a typical overworked professor. In addition to teaching, David directed CPL, where he created and grew four new national fellowship programs for graduate students. He also spoke to groups around the United States, wrote books, and provided commentary each week as a CNN senior political analyst. Nonetheless, he thought about my proposal, and suggested that each week I come to his home library, select a book, read it, and discuss it together the following week. I knew instantly that it was a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity.

What I didn’t realize initially was that each book included David’s own handwritten notes in the margins. The notes were obviously a gift for me, but they were also a source of intrigue for David, who in most cases had not revisited his initial observations, some of which were written when he was in the arena advising presidents and making history. The history he made started early in his career. His first job in the Nixon White House resulted in his penning Nixon's resignation letter.

“No one else was around,” he joked on his 80th birthday, recalling the moment to friends. The letter, addressed to Henry Kissinger, was certainly to the point. "Dear Mr. Secretary,” David Gergen wrote for Richard Nixon, "I hereby resign the Office of the President of the United States. Sincerely,’

David’s handwritten reflections in the books included many candid moments, such as “not true” next to hagiographic excerpts in books about Nixon and Johnson, and “yes!” beside content he agreed with. Passages about the importance of service were often underlined, including a passage that formed the basis of a future book David would write, and which I reference at the end of this tribute.

There were so many learnings I pulled from this inimitable deep dive on speechwriting with one of our nation's great speechwriters. A common thread across nearly all of the books was the utility of lists in speeches to distill and package ideas effectively, and to help capture attention in a cluttered world. David often affirmed this technique in our sessions together, and he recalled how he thought about it when he took over as editor of U.S. News & World Report. Under his leadership, the magazine became best known for its annual rankings lists, the most notable of which continues today to rank universities and colleges.

Whenever I deploy a list in my writing or public speaking—as I am doing at this moment—I think of David and that remarkable time in his home library with his books.

**Lesson two: you can’t influence every audience**

You can influence a lot when you have as much respect and skill as a communicator as David Gergen. But you can’t influence every audience.

I returned to Boston for a reunion of fellows of Harvard's Center for Public Leadership at the famed New England Aquarium. It was a significant occasion to celebrate CPL’s stunning growth of fellowships under David's leadership. We had the entire aquarium to ourselves, for the most part. David opened the evening at the podium.

“Good evening, fellows, friends, and family,” he began.

“Honk,” something sounded.

David paused. He raised an eyebrow. The noise stopped.

He continued. “It's an honor to be with you all. ...”

“Honk, honk.” Two penguins shuffled behind the podium, chins up.

David stopped. The penguins stopped.

He smiled, acknowledged the penguins, and began again with his remarks.

“Honk, honk, HONK.” The din grew louder.

He paused, and remarkably, the penguins stopped honking.

He continued. Gradually, as he spoke, more penguins began honking. The two penguins behind the podium appeared on top of each other.

“HONK HONK HONK.”

It was a cacophony of randy penguins, and there was nothing David Gergen could do about it.

He read the room and pivoted: “Well, that settles it then. Go forth, fellows, and multiply!” David concluded the formalities of the evening.

Years later, “honk” became a code whenever a group of us who had been in the room where it happened reconnected with David. One spectator wondered aloud if the penguins had been attracted to the timbre of his voice: baritone, smooth, and inviting.

David loves this memory. He loves to laugh.

Although David would often note that it was important to define a primary audience when writing a speech, another headline for this piece of advice could be about having a sense of humor. On this, he once wrote: “What good leaders understand is that a capacity to laugh at anxieties and absurdities of modern life won't solve your problems but will help you get through them.”
---
|
---
_A moment from the “Penguin Night” at the New England Aquarium with (L-R): Brad Davis, Rye Barcott, Jess Anderson, Max Anderson, and David Gergen. Photograph by Tom Fitzsimmons._
| **Lesson three: the past informs the future**

David often reflects on history to make more informed decisions and tell stories that inspire change. This was as true in his role advising presidents, as it was in his own writing, television commentary, and advice to mentees. One of his former research assistants referred to it as his superpower.

I benefited often from his historical wisdom, but none more so than at one of the most pivotal junctures in my career. I was considering leaving a relatively successful business career in my late 30s to start a nonprofit with Peter Dixon, a friend from the Marines who was also a mentee of David. Peter and I noticed a surge of post-9/11 veterans preparing to run for office in the 2018 midterm elections. Some of these veterans were our peers, including my business partner, Dan McCready, another Marine and Iraq War veteran. Could the next generation of veterans help lead our nation and combat the crippling polarization in Congress by working together across party lines?

Intuitively, we thought the answer was yes. We knew from our personal experiences that military service is a great equalizer. It forges bonds between Americans from all walks of life, young men and women united in service to something larger than oneself. Moreover, veteran representation in Congress had declined from over 70% to under 20% over our lifetimes as polarization in the first branch of our democracy had skyrocketed.

Nonetheless, we had no political or policymaking experience. So, I viewed David's reaction to the idea as the initial stage gate. He was the most respected bipartisan leader I knew. He cared about my career and knew many of my own strengths and shortcomings.

David listened intently as I described a character-based pledge that would form the backbone of the organization we started calling "With Honor." Any veteran we supported would need to pledge to serve with integrity, civility, and courage, I explained, especially the courage to work across party lines. We would evaluate character through veterans with whom candidates had served, many of whom had served in combat. Combat is the ultimate test of character. We would launch a political action committee that would help the most talented and competitive of the veterans run for Congress and win, especially in primary races, where veterans often faced a massive disparity in resources. After all, they had spent their early adult years in service to the nation.

Characteristically, David reacted first with historical context. He recalled working with bipartisan veterans on the national stage whose relationships together made a consequential impact, such as Senators Dan Inouye and Bob Dole. Dole and Inouye had first forged a friendship recovering together from grievous wounds in combat in WWII. He spoke also about the transformative impact of military service on Presidents Truman, Eisenhower, Kennedy, Carter, and H.W. Bush. On this theme, he would later write:

“I would not pretend the leaders of the World War II generation were perfect. They weren't. ... But on the whole that generation raised our sights about what is possible when our civic culture is strong and our leaders work across divides. On their watch, the country went to the moon, created the Peace Corps, passed major legislation advancing the causes of women and communities of color, reformed Social Security, created world-class universities, and invested heavily in science and technology. And by the way, they won the Cold War without firing a shot directly at the Russians. ... United and well led, the World War II generation proved that, when inspired, we are a can-do people.”

David brought up two of the first post-9/11 veterans who had recently been elected to Congress: Rep. Seth Moulton, whom David had mentored as a Harvard College student, and Rep. Mike Gallagher, who had earned a PhD from Princeton. I knew Seth and Mike from the Marines. They were exceptional, and I planned to call them as a next step if the meeting with David went well.

The meeting was going well. David concluded it with the essential question: had veterans in Congress been more bipartisan than non-veterans?

I didn't know the answer. There had been no outside measure of it. David encouraged me to reach out to a political science professor who helped run an analysis using data on voting in Congress and sponsorship of legislation. The result became our validation: Members of Congress with military service as a group had been more bipartisan than members of Congress without military service since World War II.

David had guided me to a narrative tied to our history and backed by data. With that, I went for it. I turned my best hours to With Honor, and asked David to join as a co-founder. He graciously agreed and months later introduced us to our largest early backer, who remembered through our mission his friend, the late U.S. Marine and Astronaut, Senator John Glenn.

---
|
---
_David and Anne Gergen at a Center for Public Leadership 2015 Reunion with mentees Marine Corps veterans (L-R) Rye Barcott, Jake Cusack, and Seth Moulton._
| **Lesson four: remember the Golden Rule**

I never actually heard David mention the Golden Rule, the moral principle that states you should treat others as you would want to be treated. Living the Golden Rule can be challenging when you have a full life with many demands on your time. For students, myself included, getting time with David could be extremely challenging. You had to really work to get on his schedule. Most students just gave up after a try or two. Some even took it personally.

“He isn't replying to my emails,” many a Harvard student thought, and at least one wrote. “I pay to go here!”

Nonetheless, if you take the time and stay politely persistent, you generally get the time. And when you get the time, he is there for you. His natural instinct is to help. He is a helper, and he is kind to people regardless of their station in life. Gracious to wait staff at dinners, taxicab drivers, custodians, strangers who wanted photos--you name it.

As the country’s polarization deepened, the commentary on cable news became louder and more obnoxious. I saw David Gergen on television hundreds of times in the past 20 years. I never once saw him lose his cool. After one particularly ridiculous moment, when David was in between two accomplished people yelling at each other, I asked him how he managed to keep his composure. He said he tried to understand where people were coming from, speak to those ideas, and not take things personally. "But it can be difficult,” he added with a laugh.

Many people know David from some level of personal interaction. Regardless of the depth of their relationship, people always speak to what a good person he is. It's remarkable. I never hear criticism, apart from the occasional quips about how to find more time with him. For those he knows well, he is an exceptional friend, the type who genuinely wants the best for you, celebrates your successes, stays present through your setbacks, and can always, always be trusted.

His kindness and concern for others carry over to his children, who are living service-oriented lives and also benefited from an amazing mother, David's wife Anne, who is a psychologist and a beautiful writer in her own right. Of Anne, David wrote in the final lines of his first book, the masterpiece _Eyewitness to Power_ :

“Public service demands its greatest sacrifices not from those in government but from their loved ones. They experience many of the hardships and few of the rewards. I was blessed that my wife, Anne, stood by me, nurtured our children in their early years, endured the long days of isolation, and, in the midst of all that, flowered into the woman she has become.”

Christopher Gergen, David's son, had told me that it would be understandable for his father to feel angry, frustrated or at least a little bit grumpy as his body and mind fight the brutal disease that is Lewy body dementia. But no, he is as kind and as gentle as ever to everyone who interacts with him, from his family to his neighbors, who include his longstanding colleague Joe Nye, a steady stream of old friends and mentees, and of course the nurses and other caregivers. David Gergen has perhaps never said the Golden rule. He just lives it.

**Lesson five: leadership at its best is service to others**

If this were a ranked-order list, this might be the first bullet for David's advice on leadership, though it is also fitting as the last. Nearly every interaction I have had with David involves the theme of service. After the first election cycle with With Honor, we helped elect or re-elect 19 members of Congress to work across party lines, including Reps. Seth Moulton and Mike Gallagher. What would this group organize around initially across party lines?

David guided us to voluntary national service, and it became one of the three key pillars of the Congressional caucus. Voluntary national service is immensely popular in polling across party lines throughout the nation. Yet fewer than 1% of young Americans now serve in the military, and fewer than 5% serve in civilian capacities or public service jobs such as teachers and nurses.

With David's encouragement and the support of many in his vast network of friends, we've since helped pass laws to expand AmeriCorps and Junior ROTC in high schools across the nation. This will be an enduring focus of commitment for With Honor.

After With Honor’s first gubernatorial candidate was elected, Maryland Governor Wes Moore, David and I flew to Baltimore to see him. “Service will save us,” Governor Moore had proclaimed on the campaign trail. We were thrilled to learn that the very first bill Wes intended to initiate focused on national service. The SERVE Act, since passed into law, created a service year option for Maryland youth.

In 2022, David published his final book. The book's title is inspired from the words of Oliver Wendell Holmes, Jr. Holmes, who had been left for dead on the battlefields of the Civil War, fought for African American rights and became one of the most consequential Supreme Court justices in U.S. history. Holmes said in a Memorial Day speech to veterans and their families in 1884:

“As life is action and passion, it is required of a man that he should share the passion and action of his time at peril of being judged not to have lived. ... Through our great good fortune, in our youth, our hearts were touched with fire. It is given to us to learn at the outset that life is a profound and passionate thing.”

David's view on service is best embodied in his own words reflecting on these lines by Holmes. David wrote in his final book, _Hearts Touched With Fire: How Great Leaders are Made:_

“What a glorious way to capture what so many young men and women have experienced in one era after another in committing themselves to civic life, seeking to create a fairer, more just, and more peaceful world. Life will hold perils, but in devoting yourself to the service of others, you find satisfaction that transcends your troubles. As many have discovered, service and leadership are inextricably bound together. Indeed, leadership at its best is service to others.”

Our nation owes a debt of gratitude to David, one of the few who makes it his life's work to serve our country and his fellow citizens. Service comes in many forms, and many of us who consider David a friend and mentor serve in uniform. But his life shows us that anyone can serve and that service can save us. Our country's brightest days can still be ahead of us through service to one another. He impresses upon us a lifetime devoted to this cause. And we, all of us who know him, are better people for knowing him. Thank you, David.
---
|
---
_February 12, 2024, Harvard Center for Public Leadership dinner after a Kennedy School Forum keynote on public service and bipartisan leadership co-hosted by With Honor with David’s colleague former Secretary of Defense Leon Panetta, who has been a champion for national service along with his son, Rep. Jimmy Panetta, who is the founding Co-Chair of the For Country Caucus. Joined by Governor Deval Patrick, David R. Gergen Professor of the Practice of Public Leadership, and Secretary Anthony Foxx, Director, Center for Public Leadership._

clean_body: | | | | | | | | | | | | | Friends, David Gergen’s son, Christopher, called With Honor Co-Founder and CEO Rye Barcott this morning to share that his father, our co-founder David Gergen, had passed away peacefully. is a link to the Washington Post obituary that just posted. Below is a tribute Rye wrote after one of his final visits with David. If you would care to contribute something in David's honor, please consider giving to the via . David’s former students created the fellowship on his 80th birthday a few years ago, and the second class of summer fellows is currently serving across the country. We will host a special tribute to David later this year at our biennial gala for the veterans we support in the 119th Congress. You can listen to his closing remarks from our 118th Gala at . | | I’m among the lucky ones to have had David Gergen as a teacher. When I graduated from Harvard Kennedy School in 2009, he became a mentor. Years later, when I started a nonprofit, he became a cofounder. Now, he is a cherished friend. An action-oriented person, I think David would most appreciate my sharing in a tribute to him advice that I learned from him that can be useful to others pursuing lives of service. Here are five pieces of advice I gained from the great good fortune of knowing David Gergen. Lesson one: use lists I might as well start with this one, perhaps the most pragmatic pieces of advice from David. Months after completing my third overseas tour as a Marine in Fallujah, Iraq, I joined the Kennedy School on a fellowship David had established with veterans in mind at Harvard's Center for Public Leadership (CPL). I knew I wanted to study under David. He was one of the few balanced voices of reason on cable news, and I had watched television anchors and commentators with keen interest ever since replying to a second-grade survey on what I wanted to be when I grew up with, "Tom Brokaw." Unfortunately, David’s class was full my first semester. We had met through a mutual friend in North Carolina, and I had a more of a personal relationship with him from the outset because we shared ties to the Old North State, where he grew up, and where I earned my bachelor's degree and intended to settle after graduation with my amazing [University of North Carolina Chapel Hill] Tar Heel wife. So, I approached him one day and proposed an independent study on speechwriting, which he had a singular point of view on, having written and edited speeches for four sitting U.S. presidents. While David had not offered an independent study focused on speechwriting before, he was interested in new ideas, appreciated my initiative, and wanted to help me with my career development. Of course, his time was stretched well beyond that of a typical overworked professor. In addition to teaching, David directed CPL, where he created and grew four new national fellowship programs for graduate students. He also spoke to groups around the United States, wrote books, and provided commentary each week as a CNN senior political analyst. Nonetheless, he thought about my proposal, and suggested that each week I come to his home library, select a book, read it, and discuss it together the following week. I knew instantly that it was a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. What I didn’t realize initially was that each book included David’s own handwritten notes in the margins. The notes were obviously a gift for me, but they were also a source of intrigue for David, who in most cases had not revisited his initial observations, some of which were written when he was in the arena advising presidents and making history. The history he made started early in his career. His first job in the Nixon White House resulted in his penning Nixon's resignation letter. “No one else was around,” he joked on his 80th birthday, recalling the moment to friends. The letter, addressed to Henry Kissinger, was certainly to the point. "Dear Mr. Secretary,” David Gergen wrote for Richard Nixon, "I hereby resign the Office of the President of the United States. Sincerely,’ David’s handwritten reflections in the books included many candid moments, such as “not true” next to hagiographic excerpts in books about Nixon and Johnson, and “yes!” beside content he agreed with. Passages about the importance of service were often underlined, including a passage that formed the basis of a future book David would write, and which I reference at the end of this tribute. There were so many learnings I pulled from this inimitable deep dive on speechwriting with one of our nation's great speechwriters. A common thread across nearly all of the books was the utility of lists in speeches to distill and package ideas effectively, and to help capture attention in a cluttered world. David often affirmed this technique in our sessions together, and he recalled how he thought about it when he took over as editor of U.S. News & World Report. Under his leadership, the magazine became best known for its annual rankings lists, the most notable of which continues today to rank universities and colleges. Whenever I deploy a list in my writing or public speaking—as I am doing at this moment—I think of David and that remarkable time in his home library with his books. Lesson two: you can’t influence every audience You can influence a lot when you have as much respect and skill as a communicator as David Gergen. But you can’t influence every audience. I returned to Boston for a reunion of fellows of Harvard's Center for Public Leadership at the famed New England Aquarium. It was a significant occasion to celebrate CPL’s stunning growth of fellowships under David's leadership. We had the entire aquarium to ourselves, for the most part. David opened the evening at the podium. “Good evening, fellows, friends, and family,” he began. “Honk,” something sounded. David paused. He raised an eyebrow. The noise stopped. He continued. “It's an honor to be with you all. ...” “Honk, honk.” Two penguins shuffled behind the podium, chins up. David stopped. The penguins stopped. He smiled, acknowledged the penguins, and began again with his remarks. “Honk, honk, HONK.” The din grew louder. He paused, and remarkably, the penguins stopped honking. He continued. Gradually, as he spoke, more penguins began honking. The two penguins behind the podium appeared on top of each other. “HONK HONK HONK.” It was a cacophony of randy penguins, and there was nothing David Gergen could do about it. He read the room and pivoted: “Well, that settles it then. Go forth, fellows, and multiply!” David concluded the formalities of the evening. Years later, “honk” became a code whenever a group of us who had been in the room where it happened reconnected with David. One spectator wondered aloud if the penguins had been attracted to the timbre of his voice: baritone, smooth, and inviting. David loves this memory. He loves to laugh. Although David would often note that it was important to define a primary audience when writing a speech, another headline for this piece of advice could be about having a sense of humor. On this, he once wrote: “What good leaders understand is that a capacity to laugh at anxieties and absurdities of modern life won't solve your problems but will help you get through them.” | A moment from the “Penguin Night” at the New England Aquarium with (L-R): Brad Davis, Rye Barcott, Jess Anderson, Max Anderson, and David Gergen. Photograph by Tom Fitzsimmons. | Lesson three: the past informs the future David often reflects on history to make more informed decisions and tell stories that inspire change. This was as true in his role advising presidents, as it was in his own writing, television commentary, and advice to mentees. One of his former research assistants referred to it as his superpower. I benefited often from his historical wisdom, but none more so than at one of the most pivotal junctures in my career. I was considering leaving a relatively successful business career in my late 30s to start a nonprofit with Peter Dixon, a friend from the Marines who was also a mentee of David. Peter and I noticed a surge of post-9/11 veterans preparing to run for office in the 2018 midterm elections. Some of these veterans were our peers, including my business partner, Dan McCready, another Marine and Iraq War veteran. Could the next generation of veterans help lead our nation and combat the crippling polarization in Congress by working together across party lines? Intuitively, we thought the answer was yes. We knew from our personal experiences that military service is a great equalizer. It forges bonds between Americans from all walks of life, young men and women united in service to something larger than oneself. Moreover, veteran representation in Congress had declined from over 70% to under 20% over our lifetimes as polarization in the first branch of our democracy had skyrocketed. Nonetheless, we had no political or policymaking experience. So, I viewed David's reaction to the idea as the initial stage gate. He was the most respected bipartisan leader I knew. He cared about my career and knew many of my own strengths and shortcomings. David listened intently as I described a character-based pledge that would form the backbone of the organization we started calling "With Honor." Any veteran we supported would need to pledge to serve with integrity, civility, and courage, I explained, especially the courage to work across party lines. We would evaluate character through veterans with whom candidates had served, many of whom had served in combat. Combat is the ultimate test of character. We would launch a political action committee that would help the most talented and competitive of the veterans run for Congress and win, especially in primary races, where veterans often faced a massive disparity in resources. After all, they had spent their early adult years in service to the nation. Characteristically, David reacted first with historical context. He recalled working with bipartisan veterans on the national stage whose relationships together made a consequential impact, such as Senators Dan Inouye and Bob Dole. Dole and Inouye had first forged a friendship recovering together from grievous wounds in combat in WWII. He spoke also about the transformative impact of military service on Presidents Truman, Eisenhower, Kennedy, Carter, and H.W. Bush. On this theme, he would later write: “I would not pretend the leaders of the World War II generation were perfect. They weren't. ... But on the whole that generation raised our sights about what is possible when our civic culture is strong and our leaders work across divides. On their watch, the country went to the moon, created the Peace Corps, passed major legislation advancing the causes of women and communities of color, reformed Social Security, created world-class universities, and invested heavily in science and technology. And by the way, they won the Cold War without firing a shot directly at the Russians. ... United and well led, the World War II generation proved that, when inspired, we are a can-do people.” David brought up two of the first post-9/11 veterans who had recently been elected to Congress: Rep. Seth Moulton, whom David had mentored as a Harvard College student, and Rep. Mike Gallagher, who had earned a PhD from Princeton. I knew Seth and Mike from the Marines. They were exceptional, and I planned to call them as a next step if the meeting with David went well. The meeting was going well. David concluded it with the essential question: had veterans in Congress been more bipartisan than non-veterans? I didn't know the answer. There had been no outside measure of it. David encouraged me to reach out to a political science professor who helped run an analysis using data on voting in Congress and sponsorship of legislation. The result became our validation: Members of Congress with military service as a group had been more bipartisan than members of Congress without military service since World War II. David had guided me to a narrative tied to our history and backed by data. With that, I went for it. I turned my best hours to With Honor, and asked David to join as a co-founder. He graciously agreed and months later introduced us to our largest early backer, who remembered through our mission his friend, the late U.S. Marine and Astronaut, Senator John Glenn. | David and Anne Gergen at a Center for Public Leadership 2015 Reunion with mentees Marine Corps veterans (L-R) Rye Barcott, Jake Cusack, and Seth Moulton. | Lesson four: remember the Golden Rule I never actually heard David mention the Golden Rule, the moral principle that states you should treat others as you would want to be treated. Living the Golden Rule can be challenging when you have a full life with many demands on your time. For students, myself included, getting time with David could be extremely challenging. You had to really work to get on his schedule. Most students just gave up after a try or two. Some even took it personally. “He isn't replying to my emails,” many a Harvard student thought, and at least one wrote. “I pay to go here!” Nonetheless, if you take the time and stay politely persistent, you generally get the time. And when you get the time, he is there for you. His natural instinct is to help. He is a helper, and he is kind to people regardless of their station in life. Gracious to wait staff at dinners, taxicab drivers, custodians, strangers who wanted photos--you name it. As the country’s polarization deepened, the commentary on cable news became louder and more obnoxious. I saw David Gergen on television hundreds of times in the past 20 years. I never once saw him lose his cool. After one particularly ridiculous moment, when David was in between two accomplished people yelling at each other, I asked him how he managed to keep his composure. He said he tried to understand where people were coming from, speak to those ideas, and not take things personally. "But it can be difficult,” he added with a laugh. Many people know David from some level of personal interaction. Regardless of the depth of their relationship, people always speak to what a good person he is. It's remarkable. I never hear criticism, apart from the occasional quips about how to find more time with him. For those he knows well, he is an exceptional friend, the type who genuinely wants the best for you, celebrates your successes, stays present through your setbacks, and can always, always be trusted. His kindness and concern for others carry over to his children, who are living service-oriented lives and also benefited from an amazing mother, David's wife Anne, who is a psychologist and a beautiful writer in her own right. Of Anne, David wrote in the final lines of his first book, the masterpiece Eyewitness to Power : “Public service demands its greatest sacrifices not from those in government but from their loved ones. They experience many of the hardships and few of the rewards. I was blessed that my wife, Anne, stood by me, nurtured our children in their early years, endured the long days of isolation, and, in the midst of all that, flowered into the woman she has become.” Christopher Gergen, David's son, had told me that it would be understandable for his father to feel angry, frustrated or at least a little bit grumpy as his body and mind fight the brutal disease that is Lewy body dementia. But no, he is as kind and as gentle as ever to everyone who interacts with him, from his family to his neighbors, who include his longstanding colleague Joe Nye, a steady stream of old friends and mentees, and of course the nurses and other caregivers. David Gergen has perhaps never said the Golden rule. He just lives it. Nearly every interaction I have had with David involves the theme of service. What would this group organize around initially across party lines? David guided us to voluntary national service, and it became one of the three key pillars of the Congressional caucus. Voluntary national service is immensely popular in polling across party lines throughout the nation. Yet fewer than 1% of young Americans now serve in the military, and fewer than 5% serve in civilian capacities or public service jobs such as teachers and nurses. With David's encouragement and the support of many in his vast network of friends, we've since helped pass laws to expand AmeriCorps and Junior ROTC in high schools across the nation. This will be an enduring focus of commitment for With Honor. After With Honor’s first gubernatorial candidate was elected, Maryland Governor Wes Moore, David and I flew to Baltimore to see him. “Service will save us,” Governor Moore had proclaimed on the campaign trail. We were thrilled to learn that the very first bill Wes intended to initiate focused on national service. The SERVE Act, since passed into law, created a service year option for Maryland youth. Through our great good fortune, in our youth, our hearts were touched with fire. And we, all of us who know him, are better people for knowing him. body:
We’re mapping the path to Election Day

| [](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/111331599/556873768/-793144256?nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMDYvMS84NDIzNSIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICI3ZmM0ZjIwMy1hMjVlLWYwMTEtOGRjOS02MDQ1YmRmZThlOWMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=mlkKfSbiw3-kKxqcLgCzUa75on2IB2nWTNm2tWgALf0=&emci=71c4f203-a25e-f011-8dc9-6045bdfe8e9c&emdi=7fc4f203-a25e-f011-8dc9-6045bdfe8e9c&ceid=15759664)

Hey Derek, Now that the primary is finally behind us and Q2 is officially in the books, it’s time for us to look ahead, and our sights are set on one big goal: a massive Democratic win in November. **To keep ourselves oriented, we made the Roadmap to Election Day, so we all know when early voting starts, when we’re coming up on a fundraising deadline, or when it’s time to make sure your registration is updated. name: Senator Vincent Hughes
subject: Senator Hughes and Sheryl Lee Ralph Celebrate Summer Reading
body:
If you are having trouble viewing this message or would like to share it on a social network, you can [view the message online](http://enews.senatorhughes.com/mail/util.cfm?gpiv=2100170326.172789.35&gen=1).

---|---
[](http://enews.senatorhughes.com/mail/util.cfm?mailaction=clickthru&gpiv=2100170326.172789.35&gen=1&mailing_linkid=15000)
# **Senator Hughes and Sheryl Lee Ralph Celebrate Summer Reading with Read 2 Succeed **
Senator Hughes and his wife, activist and actress, Sheryl Lee Ralph [joined students and teachers](http://enews.senatorhughes.com/mail/util.cfm?mailaction=clickthru&gpiv=2100170326.172789.35&gen=1&mailing_linkid=15001) at the [Read 2 Succeed program](http://enews.senatorhughes.com/mail/util.cfm?mailaction=clickthru&gpiv=2100170326.172789.35&gen=1&mailing_linkid=15003) at Overbrook Elementary to [give out hundreds](http://enews.senatorhughes.com/mail/util.cfm?mailaction=clickthru&gpiv=2100170326.172789.35&gen=1&mailing_linkid=15004) of Abbott Elementary books and celebrate summer reading. Read 2 Succeed was founded in 2012 by Senator Hughes to provide students an opportunity to keep learning in the summer months, for free! In 2024, Read 2 Succeed launched a year-long program that offers students and families access to reading materials and instruction to foster continuous literacy growth throughout the year. Since it was founded, Read 2 Succeed has distributed over 10,000 books and served over 2,500 children. Senator Hughes and his wife are committed to continuing to support the program and ensure that students have access to learning opportunities that help them prepare for a bright, limitless future!
| Offices of Senator Vincent Hughes

**Delta Rescue**
Delta Rescue is one the largest no-kill animal sanctuaries. Leo Grillo is on a mission to help all abandoned, malnourished, hurt or suffering animals. He relies solely on contributions from people like you and me. If you want to help Leo to continue his mission of running one of the best care-for-life animal sanctuaries in the country please visit Delta Rescue at: https://deltarescue.org/
**Firecracker Farms**
Everything's better with HOT SALT. Firecracker Farms hot salt is hand crafted on their family farm with Carolina Reaper, Ghost and Trinidad Scorpion peppers. This is a balanced, deep flavor pairs perfect with your favorite foods. Whether it's eggs, steaks veggies or even your favorite beverage, Firecracker Farms hot salt is what you've been missing. Just head to https://firecracker.farm/ use code word: SEAN for a discount. Unlock the flavor in your food now!
Victor Davis Hanson joins me today to discuss all that President Trump and his administration have accomplished in these first 6 months. The cabinet meeting earlier this week really highlighted how the administration is firing on all cylinders. Victor draws comparisons to Winston Churchill, he stood defiant to Hitler and Russia and all of Europe were folding and making deals with the German dictator. Winston was alone in his defiance and when he returned to power, everything changed. President Trump is similar in his defiance and in his return to power has changed our country in just 6 short months. Democrats on the other hand are embracing Socialism, Zohran Mamdani is just the latest whose parents left impoverished countries for a better life in America. Like Mamdani, Barack Obama, Kamala Harris and Rep. Ilhan Omar reflect the pattern of affluent, left-wing, highly educated politicians that succeeded under the safety and rule of law of America; yet seek to destroy and replace the American way of life with a Communist society.

**Victor Davis Hanson**

name: Laura Gillen
subject: They knew exactly what they were doing
disclaimer_text: Paid for and authorized by Gillen for NY
body:
If we don’t hold this seat, we lose our chance to take back the House and stop their dangerous agenda.

| Peter,

Republicans knew exactly what they were doing when they voted for their Big Ugly Bill and _they did it anyway._

They knew it would kick millions off their health insurance.

They knew it would slash food assistance.

And, they knew it would reward billionaires and corporations with tax breaks they don’t need.

But they voted for it anyway. Because this bill wasn’t about policy. It was about pleasing Trump and protecting their donors. [_**We cannot let them win this seat back. body:
That’s how much AIPAC and its affiliates spent on elections across the country in the 2024 election cycle.
# [](https://act.ilhanomar.com/go/35?t=1&akid=1709%2E791427%2Es19Ypf)

**$89,708,313.**
That’s how much AIPAC and its affiliates spent on elections across the country in the 2024 election cycle.
Yes, you read that number right. Over $89 million.
Lots of that money went towards unseating progressive members of Congress who stand up for Palestinian rights and self-determination.
Millions spent on vicious attack ads, mailers, and more. Millions spent to divide the Democratic party when we should be focused on Trump and the far-right.
**$3.**
That’s how much we are asking you to contribute to Ilhan Omar’s reelection campaign today.
With AIPAC testing the waters of running a candidate against Ilhan — a candidate who would have the backing of their huge warchest — we have to take this threat seriously.
We know $3 doesn’t sound like much in the face of millions and millions of dollars, but trust us. It really does add up when everyone does their part.
That’s how we’ve driven Ilhan to victory in three reelection campaigns so far, and how we’ll do it again now.
So please: **[Chip in $3 today and help our campaign prepare for the tough fights ahead. We know we can win this race once again, but it’s going to take the help of everyone who believes in Ilhan’s mission and is ready to join the fight to defend our progressive values.**](https://act.ilhanomar.com/go/7?c0=io-em-july25.Ih7rVK&refcode=em250711-1-full&t=2&akid=1709%2E791427%2Es19Ypf)

body:
He even wished a barbaric fate on Elon Musk, saying he’d love to see him “dragged out of the building with his hair plugs and lynched on the street.”
[View in Web Browser](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.com/click/e29909023/VaHR0cHM6Ly93cm8ubnVjbGV1c2VtYWlsLmNvbS9hbXBsaWZ5L3YvSFFnZ2c0eDdzVw/SZW1haWxfYW1lcmljYU9ORXN0d2Fycm9vbV9jb20yNTA3MTE/LbmNsOVJEU0s5TA/qP2hpZHM9bmNCNzBJN1g5aHg3WXJscw/gaHGPSA/sag5b630b0a)

| | | | [****](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.)
Dear Patriot,

Summer is here, and with it, distractions abound. But at **O'Keefe Media Group** , we never take a break from exposing the truth – and [**we need your help to keep the momentum going**](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.).

**Donations are down this July** , and [**without your support**](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.), our critical investigations could grind to a halt.

Recently, our undercover journalists caught **Arslan Akhtar** , a U.S. State Department visa specialist, on hidden camera making shocking admissions.

[****](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.)
Akhtar confessed to coaching illegal immigrants on how to dodge ICE, telling them, **“Don’t talk to the police, don’t admit the truth, keep your mouth shut.”**

He even bragged about reversing rejected visas to help applicants gain entry into the U.S.

[**Watch our latest report and donate here to keep us going.**](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.)

But it gets worse. Akhtar openly expressed hatred for entire groups of people – Yemenis, Indians, Bangladeshis, and white Americans – calling them “awful” and “garbage.”

He even wished a barbaric fate on Elon Musk, saying he’d love to see him “dragged out of the building with his hair plugs and lynched on the street.”

**This is a man with power over who gets to enter our country.**

I believe the American people deserve to know what’s happening behind closed doors.

**Our hidden cameras revealed Akhtar’s bias and abuse of power** , and we’re not stopping there.

But with summer distractions pulling donations away, we’re at risk of falling short. **[**We need to raise $53,000 by July 31st to keep our operations running and continue exposing corruption.**](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.)**

Your gift today will fund:

* **More undercover investigations** to uncover corruption in government and institutions.

* **Cutting-edge equipment** to capture undeniable evidence.

* **Legal defense** against lawsuits, like the one from Washington attorney Mark Zaid, who’s trying to silence our work.

**[**Will you step up with a tax-deductible donation today**](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.)****?**

Every dollar brings us closer to holding the powerful accountable. **[**Click here to donate securely now: O'Keefe Media Group Donation Page**](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.)**

We’re fighting for truth, and **we’re fighting for you**.

We will not stop exposing the truth... because you, the American people, deserve to be informed.”

** _Join us in this mission. Donate today._**

In Truth,
James O’Keefe
Founder and CEO
O'Keefe Media Group

P.S. **Summer slowdowns could stop our work in its tracks.**[**Your gift of $25, $50, $100, or more**](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.) will ensure we can keep exposing corruption like Arslan Akhtar’s. [**Donate now and help us keep the truth alive!**](https://link.email.america1stwarroom.)

**WASHINGTON, D.C.** – U.S. Senator Katie Britt (R-AL) led a bipartisan group of lawmakers in advancing historic updates to federal child care tax credits, included in the Senate’s recently passed reconciliation bill. The measure, which passed by a narrow 51-50 vote, marks the first major revision to key child care tax credits in over two decades.

The legislation modernizes and expands the Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit (CDCTC), Dependent Care Assistance Program (DCAP), and the Employer-Provided Child Care Tax Credit (45F). While CDCTC and 45F had not been permanently updated since 2001, DCAP had not been revised since 1986. Advocates say these changes are long overdue as the cost of child care has risen by 263% over that time period.

Earlier this week, the U.S. Senate passed “Lulu’s Law”—a piece of legislation sponsored by U.S. Senator Katie Britt, R-Alabama, which would create a national warning alert system for shark attacks through the Federal Communications Commission.

The law is named for 16-year-old Lulu Gribbin from Mountain Brook, Alabama, who lost her left hand and right leg in a shark attack on a Florida beach last year. The Alabama state Legislature passed its own version of Lulu’s Law during the 2025 Legislative Session, which established a state shark alert system for Baldwin and Mobile counties.

During a Tuesday appearance on Fox News Channel's "Hannity," U.S. Sen. Katie Britt (R-Montgomery) criticized Democrats for blaming President Donald Trump and his administration for the recent Texas flooding, which has reportedly eclipsed a death toll of 100.

After offering up prayers and support, noting that a Mountain Brook youth and Mobile family had been lost in the devastation, Britt blasted Democrats such as Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) for saying National Weather Service cuts led to the disaster.

clean_body: | | | | | Official Team Britt Newsletter | | WASHINGTON, D.C. – U.S. Senator Katie Britt (R-AL) led a bipartisan group of lawmakers in advancing historic updates to federal child care tax credits, included in the Senate’s recently passed reconciliation bill. The measure, which passed by a narrow 51-50 vote, marks the first major revision to key child care tax credits in over two decades. The legislation modernizes and expands the Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit (CDCTC), Dependent Care Assistance Program (DCAP), and the Employer-Provided Child Care Tax Credit (45F). While CDCTC and 45F had not been permanently updated since 2001, DCAP had not been revised since 1986. Advocates say these changes are long overdue as the cost of child care has risen by 263% over that time period. | | Earlier this week, the U.S. Senate passed “Lulu’s Law”—a piece of legislation sponsored by U.S. Senator Katie Britt, R-Alabama, which would create a national warning alert system for shark attacks through the Federal Communications Commission. The law is named for 16-year-old Lulu Gribbin from Mountain Brook, Alabama, who lost her left hand and right leg in a shark attack on a Florida beach last year. The Alabama state Legislature passed its own version of Lulu’s Law during the 2025 Legislative Session, which established a state shark alert system for Baldwin and Mobile counties. | | During a Tuesday appearance on Fox News Channel's "Hannity," U.S. Sen. Katie Britt (R-Montgomery) criticized Democrats for blaming President Donald Trump and his administration for the recent Texas flooding, which has reportedly eclipsed a death toll of 100. After offering up prayers and support, noting that a Mountain Brook youth and Mobile family had been lost in the devastation, Britt blasted Democrats such as Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) for saying National Weather Service cuts led to the disaster. | | | | | | | | | Gulf of America Hat Celebrate conservative leadership and show your patriotism with this unique, high quality, Made-in-America hat. | | Follow Katie On Social Media: | | | ---|---|--- SIGN UP FOR SMS ALERTS: TEXT "KATIE" TO 27488 By texting KATIE, you consent to join a recurring SMS/MMS text messaging program that will provide alerts, donation requests, updates, and other important information. body:
Congressional Republicans just pushed through a bill that feels like something out of a bad dream.

Gabe Vasquez won the most competitive Democratic seat in Congress in 2022. He's running to be a champion for working families and build an economy that benefits everyone. If you’d like to unsubscribe, [click here.](https://secure.ngpvan.com/p/lNYIdkhPikOBrh6RGc-KWg2?unsubscribedata=W6yoiSjahukVXwjWp88a2o7CTLkoW7PqDzzKwl1PhLXfWvoenIn6SEKrGXV6c7yes5ZhBma%2B43hV7Vg0vKjQLVlxlLh3T3fQ5td37nVjB3%2F4URvtD%2FboW0%2BGj6E9awhaAIvQ5C%2BCnxzz0IaKvt8pI9xCM1ijG8vfBzK6EII4se7kxlYAU4KJjOqH5sPTqXCboPxfNmB%2FfeR8qnjkNzwo%2BEqwk9ctN19mXb9%2B4iBlWa7Ja7nN3ChJq16AEKVjt4UiweCp%2BlNOJCHj1DTvfTOMTVdO5OilBbhUxnxYBcGZgTltaGudZSHwMobkKPzsIdA1VNpd6fWZ8urYpm4VERIa%2FtAyD4XD5LxdSn%2BCCDPrl6FWGVvIcu1QkVq2itWRr5oYMRYY9URHq18s2KpaaF7eB3b7zieXcgqpHv76FW4z0gY%3D)

Earlier this month, Congressional Republicans pushed through a bill that honestly feels like something out of a bad dream. I’ve spent a lot of time across southern New Mexico sitting down with families who rely on Medicaid to get to the doctor or use SNAP to make sure their kids don’t go to bed hungry. These programs aren’t a waste — they’re essentials. They’re what help people stay healthy, put food on the table, and work toward something better. But this new bill? It slashes those programs. It guts Medicaid. It cuts food assistance. And it does it knowing full well the harm it will cause. Why? So they can hand out even more tax breaks to billionaires and special interests. Think of it like a reverse Robin Hood — taking from the people who have the least and giving it to those who already have the most. It’s cruel and it’s wrong. **And I’m going to keep fighting like hell against it.** But here’s the truth: I can’t do it alone. This district could be the deciding seat in the fight to flip the House. If we win here, we can finally start holding Donald Trump and his enablers accountable. And Republicans know it. That’s why they’re already flooding this race with dark money and doing whatever it takes to try to buy this seat. I love New Mexico. I love our people. I see how hard folks here work every single day to care for their families, their neighbors, and their future. We deserve better than what this bill will do to us. I’ve spent a lot of time across southern New Mexico sitting down with families who rely on Medicaid to get to the doctor or use SNAP to make sure their kids don’t go to bed hungry. These programs aren’t a waste — they’re essentials. They’re what help people stay healthy, put food on the table, and work toward something better. But this new bill? It slashes those programs. It guts Medicaid. It cuts food assistance. And it does it knowing full well the harm it will cause. Why? So they can hand out even more tax breaks to billionaires and special interests. Think of it like a reverse Robin Hood — taking from the people who have the least and giving it to those who already have the most. It’s cruel and it’s wrong. And I’m going to keep fighting like hell against it. But here’s the truth: I can’t do it alone. This district could be the deciding seat in the fight to flip the House. If we win here, we can finally start holding Donald Trump and his enablers accountable. And Republicans know it. That’s why they’re already flooding this race with dark money and doing whatever it takes to try to buy this seat. I love New Mexico. I love our people. I see how hard folks here work every single day to care for their families, their neighbors, and their future. We deserve better than what this bill will do to us. = | | = We’re in this together — and I truly believe that if we keep showing up for each other, we’re going to get through this. — Gabe | | | ---|---|--- = Together, we flipped NM-02 in 2022. Now, Gabe is busy in Congress fighting for reproductive choice, economic opportunity, affordable healthcare, and the best interests of the people. Gabe won the most competitive Democratic seat in Congress in 2022, and is running to be a champion for working families and build an economy that benefits everyone.** | = = Paid for by Gabe Vasquez for Congress Gabe Vasquez for Congress Drawer L Mesilla, NM 88046 United States If you would like to unsubscribe, click here: To receive less email from Gabe Vasquez for Congress, . You can also =.

Leader Jeffries is calling EVERY Democrat to action — but we’re still missing critical support in your state.

House Republicans have been nothing more than puppets for Donald Trump: GUTTING Medicaid, THREATENING Social Security, and making Trump a KING!!

I’m so grateful to have you leading the charge to defend our democracy, and for never backing down in the face of MAGA attacks.

**So today, I’m answering your call to action to make sure we flip the House in the midterms and STOP Trump in his tracks!**

name: Marc Elias
subject: We're suing Donald Trump
disclaimer_text: Paid for by the DSCC | PO Box 96047, Washington, DC 20090 | **[dscc
body:
| | | Hi friend, it's Marc Elias. **I beat Donald Trump 64 times in court. And now, working with the DSCC,_I'll beat him again._**

[**Before I tell you more, will you chip in $5 to the DSCC to help power the work to stop Trump's allies and take back the Senate majority? >>**](https://go.dscc.org/go/21844?amount=5&t=5&firstname=&lastname=&email=dpwillis67%40gmail.com&addr1=&addr2=&city=Cresco&state=Pennsylvania&zip=18326&phone=None&employer=&occupation=&ask=5&amounts=5%2C10%2C20%2C50&code=DPE-FD-20250711-MarcElias-EliasSuingTrump-Pros-sl1&refcode=DPE-FD-20250711-MarcElias-EliasSuingTrump-Pros-sl1&akid=20725%2E9716520%2ErRR9ya)

Right now, Donald Trump is attacking our longstanding values and principles. He's ignoring due process, flaunting anti-corruption laws, and, worst of all, **attacking our democracy and voting rights -- the very bedrock of American society.**

It's disgusting, but not surprising from the guy who claimed he wanted to be a dictator on his first day in office.

That's where my team and the DSCC come in -- the very moment Trump signed an executive order to interfere in our elections and threaten the rights of voters, we _immediately_ filed suit against him on behalf of Democrats across the nation. And we've already notched some key victories in the case!

**But the fight isn't over.** We all know that Trump and his allies will keep trying to break American democracy. We can't let them, so we need ALL Democrats -- from leaders like Barack and Michelle Obama to supporters like you -- working together.

name: Jon Ossoff
subject: The one race MAGA is banking on
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Warren for Senate
body:
| | | [ ](https://act.elizabethwarren.com/go/216?t=3&akid=2591%2E4032634%2E88POMX)
---
Elizabeth Warren said I could reach out, because this U.S. Senate race in Georgia isn’t just another election. It’s the one race MAGA is banking on. See, as the only Democratic Senator up for re-election in a state Trump won, I am, quite literally, their #1 target. [**You know the stakes. And you know your support is crucial. If you are ready to make a donation today, please split a donation between my campaign and Elizabeth Warren today.**](https://act.elizabethwarren.com/go/3055?t=8&akid=2591%2E4032634%2E88POMX)

---
_If you've saved payment info with ActBlue Express, your donation will process automatically:_ |
---
| [DONATE NOW: $3](https://act.elizabethwarren.com/go/3055?t=22&express_lane=true&amount=3&akid=2591%2E4032634%2E88POMX)
---
| [DONATE NOW: $15](https://act.elizabethwarren.com/go/3055?t=24&express_lane=true&amount=15&akid=2591%2E4032634%2E88POMX)
---
| [DONATE NOW: $28](https://act.elizabethwarren.com/go/3055?t=26&express_lane=true&amount=28&akid=2591%2E4032634%2E88POMX)
---
| [DONATE NOW: $50](https://act.elizabethwarren.com/go/3055?t=28&express_lane=true&amount=50&akid=2591%2E4032634%2E88POMX)
---
| [DONATE NOW: $100](https://act.elizabethwarren.com/go/3055?t=18&express_lane=true&amount=100&akid=2591%2E4032634%2E88POMX)
---
| [OTHER AMOUNT](https://act.elizabethwarren.com/go/3055?t=20&express_lane=true&akid=2591%2E4032634%2E88POMX)
---

---
Otherwise, I hope you’ll give me a minute or two to explain why your support is so important. They’re going to come after me with every dirty trick in the book and will spare no expense to defeat me. That means we need to build the largest statewide campaign Georgia has ever seen. That means more rallies. More organizers. More outreach — and early outreach — to voters in every corner of the state. All of that takes resources. And it’s why donations from supporters like you are so important to our campaign. The road ahead won’t be easy. But I know how to win tough fights. I’ve done it before. I can’t do it alone, which is why I’m asking: [**Please, consider splitting a contribution between my re-election and Elizabeth Warren. Every donation counts in a race as close as this one will be.**](https://act.elizabethwarren.com/go/3055?t=14&akid=2591%2E4032634%2E88POMX) Thanks for all you do to support our campaign. As always, Jon

Peter,

Right here in NC-04, we’re beginning to recover from the flooding after Tropical Storm Chantal made landfall last weekend, and our friends in Texas were struck by a deadly and catastrophic flood — **we can no longer deny the consequences of climate change.**

The consistent onslaught of natural disasters we are facing is a blazing indicator of what’s to come. Climate change is an imminent threat to our planet, our home, and our future.

If we fail to pass legislation that protects our planet, we will continue to suffer. We must do everything we can to preserve and restore our climate — for us, for our children, for future generations. **There is no _“Planet B”_ , Peter. **

[I refuse to stand by as our neighbors face another tragedy. I refuse to stand by as our nation fails to address the reality of the climate crisis. Please join me in fighting for climate action by signing the petition below.](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/111333067/556878747/-1451867996?source=SBD_VF_EM_PET_071125_Climate_180DO_D1_S1&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMzMvMS85NTc5MyIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJjMGI1ZGI5NS1hODVlLWYwMTEtOGRjOS02MDQ1YmRmZThlOWMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=lzhUCKyjsim9_h7lQ0xdmrpbxVtPc1LUaG6kMTiIytU=&emci=b4b5db95-a85e-f011-8dc9-6045bdfe8e9c&emdi=c0b5db95-a85e-f011-8dc9-6045bdfe8e9c&ceid=799895) | [ADD YOUR NAME](https://click.ngpvan.com/k/111333068/556878748/-1451867996?source=SBD_VF_EM_PET_071125_Climate_180DO_D1_S1&nvep=ew0KICAiVGVuYW50VXJpIjogIm5ncHZhbjovL3Zhbi9OR1AvTkdQMzMvMS85NTc5MyIsDQogICJEaXN0cmlidXRpb25VbmlxdWVJZCI6ICJjMGI1ZGI5NS1hODVlLWYwMTEtOGRjOS02MDQ1YmRmZThlOWMiLA0KICAiRW1haWxBZGRyZXNzIjogImRwd2lsbGlzNjdAZ21haWwuY29tIg0KfQ%3D%3D&hmac=lzhUCKyjsim9_h7lQ0xdmrpbxVtPc1LUaG6kMTiIytU=&emci=b4b5db95-a85e-f011-8dc9-6045bdfe8e9c&emdi=c0b5db95-a85e-f011-8dc9-6045bdfe8e9c&ceid=799895)

Now more than ever, we must strengthen our environmental protection laws and advocate for sustainable development. When we demand action, we create solutions. When we establish progress, we protect our future. Thank you, Valerie * | | | ---|---|--- = Valerie is fighting to protect access to health care, working families, and to reform our criminal justice system. But she needs your help to continue to do this great work in Congress. Your donation right now will help protect Democrat Valerie Foushee by giving us a jumpstart on the next cycle. | = = Paid for by Foushee for Congress Valerie Foushee for Congress PO Box 16446 Chapel Hill, NC 27516 United States If you need to you can unsubscribe, You can also =.

With the fast-paced nature of campaign life, I’m putting out new fires every day, but there is one thing I am always happy to see when I’m pulling stats to show Marlene: **Recurring donations.** Recurring donations are the backbone of any good fundraising program, but more than that, they are a sign of your commitment to sending new leadership to Washington, D.C. This month, so far, 15% of our donations are thanks to our monthly donors — our La Fuerza Monthly program. 15% is amazing all on its own. disclaimer_text: PAID FOR BY TUBERVILLE FOR GOVERNOR, INC
body:
I know what competition looks like. I understand what fairness means. | [ ](http://links.tubervilleforalabama.com/u/click?_t=b8b4efbe79f64b579dfa7d8da685a7a9&_m=48e85192c204403fb9de4978132b10c8&_e=VXQbmJx5Lsy_POK2HbOpuoHRxaPlO5p4qydzmKViis-BKrhXiBsshBvfLjKaGKoE5rLdYLZfLFt76pOtPzi8nlOeLZLBTphoBTVQ7VDlCOFUOLYCGzhKe0-pWEQTMdI_gS-Nhnla8Ui09Q_-cMJec7-7ZCC5N2z-eQqnpgD5gMN0rZKmGYY3_ed6BRaTn_DWhriEnh-Bs7vLEjYePOMeZKsGo13WZvxJhaq9ITgG5tAocgynbOmOvUeme3gtKBYoIsikCYM7gkKXGuJzqg41mQTdxPI7wmXnXmW2xTyJiUCNTgOsCLJwOD8GMHRzf8p9SzQIbluqqusEJzjhq5dLPFMIG-_Y_FLwdSb5l5xKzDGLUqnVUHdanQPY6kDzopwje7Noe0iMcqgR7lvXoj8UgAMm8s3mzX8ZHYPC2ITDjCPwA7L1dTOWEWAyZvc42DRFs-SfsYLWE5sbe6CUnqa8BBtj2f0hpSIbEEvh14vKSKC9YG4-p_1pTb5-GosClKGdYlPjStBRQFoYk5Zr3uVMtCOHmvikJG8qC00nSeD80Lk04aTdEtOrI42LSthM2vdn)

Peter,

Most Americans know the truth: **Men do NOT belong in women’s sports. Period.**

Even President Trump signed an Executive Order to prohibit it.

But here’s the kicker...

Blue states are acting like “sanctuary cities” for men who want to throw on a skirt, invade women’s locker rooms, and dominate the competition.

[](http://links.tubervilleforalabama.com/u/click?_t=b8b4efbe79f64b579dfa7d8da685a7a9&_m=48e85192c204403fb9de4978132b10c8&_e=VXQbmJx5Lsy_POK2HbOpuoHRxaPlO5p4qydzmKViis-BKrhXiBsshBvfLjKaGKoE5rLdYLZfLFt76pOtPzi8nlOeLZLBTphoBTVQ7VDlCOFUOLYCGzhKe0-pWEQTMdI_gS-Nhnla8Ui09Q_-cMJec1xSPmysJGm4xBqTMg3DG6TMgRrKQx7H5TNoNpK-g-Qa5UF6vHQusYFMAKT8Q-6n9rh4F8yR_dFiNg5ZmttJb3YZpQ5ekJurA305LGv2hcA4EEB_SpkWQwuIOPWkBRJ_8wbf8w0YiDcZBsgfmM8B3cQmbQX8Kuz8bZZPfKHjBXWkk6CZRutK7yRnv6wPlAUoyv4EFKNGYhG31NrdtKm2ye8qx7qidQzISrBKaiB56Fl3gwa3LMluCPRz0alxdGIEL6zEFH94WRaoRp3islh0ummX6EckdYOyUC0MsrTffE3_ZmKeyjHiNEfQqTt9aH0jmHRZCQhTpSO7dBX0ckXY3cU0Lt95e6rPlw3eQYwd0HwFMR9W88_mQPgvOyL1JhC5U-SiLnsOuYRyak8t0rO_I5w%3D)

**BEYOND ridiculous** , it’s not just unfair. It’s dangerous and insulting to every young woman who trains her whole life for a fair shot.

**[I’m fighting back, but I need your support.](http://links.tubervilleforalabama.com/u/click?_t=b8b4efbe79f64b579dfa7d8da685a7a9&_m=48e85192c204403fb9de4978132b10c8&_e=VXQbmJx5Lsy_POK2HbOpuoHRxaPlO5p4qydzmKViis-BKrhXiBsshBvfLjKaGKoE5rLdYLZfLFt76pOtPzi8nlOeLZLBTphoBTVQ7VDlCOFUOLYCGzhKe0-pWEQTMdI_gS-Nhnla8Ui09Q_-cMJec7-7ZCC5N2z-eQqnpgD5gMN0rZKmGYY3_ed6BRaTn_DWgRfPmzSitFnhnegbmcXiLe1q3XDWgLOjhnAQELHWmqhDW7CcQ7aixIywJYpMl504rJPxNVJhEiV_v2u0krCnnLg9QqD4tNttAZdFwa18sP00482B3f1365CpFY4bYC5bbhBP2FTtWRKNhNvP007MFFFyZK49Z5e-BO2qGpyLnCej7uI-vVSv5RF875KrI5TvF49ckXjbk8xuqWW9B8DkowIJosGCUlKQuWwtia605GhkWpWK4nsKz05Q0tWWhU8pjoi3wKMME0MgQA5NdotYAEAO_eVNwJQ0N7IApVZNIGVoBG-lP7ZrTTe-hKxqy9u92cPqwRGkMwNU7Bih83Cfmw0_wG-PNSD2n6my6FT2Uj8bUIkx57TQB6r5eScLMFyA)**

If you believe in common sense, fairness, and women’s rights, will you stand with me right now?

| [ **STAND WITH COACH TUBERVILLE** ](http://links.tubervilleforalabama.com/u/click?_t=b8b4efbe79f64b579dfa7d8da685a7a9&_m=48e85192c204403fb9de4978132b10c8&_e=VXQbmJx5Lsy_POK2HbOpuoHRxaPlO5p4qydzmKViis-BKrhXiBsshBvfLjKaGKoE5rLdYLZfLFt76pOtPzi8nlOeLZLBTphoBTVQ7VDlCOFUOLYCGzhKe0-pWEQTMdI_gS-Nhnla8Ui09Q_-cMJec7-7ZCC5N2z-eQqnpgD5gMN0rZKmGYY3_ed6BRaTn_DWXpXbEIDFUHXUUxRpfK-8VxZ5PnYISyIRUdl0HPszIuQb0xx_UbHT9x9v1SRm-ebIQfhXzd13pgAT80SKtrD-hhhRQGnx4h8GrRAoVYgODqJqE-G7Hryf411ugs_97lHuxjM2Y7C9QCbAvKkxEfEisEMGL6hGSZaxz2ecUwEpkCM0VjZ3FCZ9pplNRD9l8DUZasN_s4kjGNaXlhyAUcOOdfdGHEyXaWT3ZF4dTakBoHHpfpLG2MtUzdNGoBgsUyid5FvyTqKGAqS2M39_PuKdOppoH_tvfjWtP3-GcOyCS0r84qnXIjHaAVGGxmPh9zs7ZBHO7_polibcSNf63zsG0SVnmsqC3NDF-QhYq_0Vaktz7G9plvT7xT2wLscZPjxJ)
---

As a former college football coach, I’ve spent decades in locker rooms, on sidelines, and in weight rooms. **I know what competition looks like. I understand what fairness means.**

And what’s happening right now? It ain’t fair, and it ain’t right.

The Left can’t define a woman. I’ll never stop fighting to protect our girls, daughters, and athletes. **[Let’s send a message they can’t ignore.](http://links.tubervilleforalabama.com/u/click?_t=b8b4efbe79f64b579dfa7d8da685a7a9&_m=48e85192c204403fb9de4978132b10c8&_e=VXQbmJx5Lsy_POK2HbOpuoHRxaPlO5p4qydzmKViis-BKrhXiBsshBvfLjKaGKoE5rLdYLZfLFt76pOtPzi8nlOeLZLBTphoBTVQ7VDlCOFUOLYCGzhKe0-pWEQTMdI_gS-Nhnla8Ui09Q_-cMJec7-7ZCC5N2z-eQqnpgD5gMN0rZKmGYY3_ed6BRaTn_DWEyMYUHrM4y5x2gTo2rPQjYed4ggkPHm1yLr9_Wz0cR2Is_8qpt69_o4CGkKkdCpUMe7jI-WOrEfvk4WZvhJoKAtYO8Ry7qsa0gGehFuZFC2nRed7aDzyum9xtmiMGRYRQKSoJQx5sLV6ESEQj7z8wHA8wsN0wh-ZscaZB6nxUFlvgVrecgbGiKDZhLe2c15U5Up4kY7qlOTSKeX-yp5DSYVjYV4qtGb0o7p2lBXZdvTgLWjlEq_iuqvTsJC7yC3JdmG06U50W6LR58r_AKI3PG79VbWYCIA7nqtaTTlRSweZTtaiIA0k6J1lBrSDqjfth9wryAs4sVdvEYW72J1awd7xZ1wWeSmxfaCFJWhCNNdx0IbvALMIixIZk1-kvSz4)**

For America,

Coach Tuberville

[](http://links.tubervilleforalabama.com/u/click?_t=b8b4efbe79f64b579dfa7d8da685a7a9&_m=48e85192c204403fb9de4978132b10c8&_e=VXQbmJx5Lsy_POK2HbOpuoHRxaPlO5p4qydzmKViis-BKrhXiBsshBvfLjKaGKoE5rLdYLZfLFt76pOtPzi8nlOeLZLBTphoBTVQ7VDlCOFUOLYCGzhKe0-pWEQTMdI_gS-Nhnla8Ui09Q_-cMJec7-7ZCC5N2z-eQqnpgD5gMN0rZKmGYY3_ed6BRaTn_DWBNBWidOx1Rs9XhEG6K443rPCUx8XAmH42HTJVJM2d1jDnzVCdtuMbGpCsn1i99ZszN0uhLctQc2YRmS3qdMmYP-ztbH6dKH5HDUhobkneDuqYcxfzIUT_qmLbuPVugr5iYyPraPEzNHeBOuzaEjyklD8Oz-2osqV5FxPKfIQmxmvjdQY6Z9UmIz67ds5Kb1CPf24KlNxHxiZxVl_-O4vHKiZXaqt69kv3ez2NNS9ySAkosMQgK3V2KQz0A1ffXOFIounHB22rSxK2V1CAi7Hvtf6oq7Y5X9N5rQUXdtY2TiTELytxXtbDQtoU5JJXe7wM6leyzXn5Kwfv6qpC1WZeblfyfJ8e_PlFuOd9Ket39tX0jP4kpKY6xj42HLkQ832)

Meet Coach Tuberville:

**Coach Tuberville was born in Camden, Arkansas, the third of three children of Olive and Charles Tuberville. College Football’s 2004 Coach of the Year and two-time SEC Coach of the Year, Tommy Tuberville is most often recognized for his success on the field. But it is the legacy of his father, a highly decorated WWII veteran and Purple Heart recipient, that motivates Coach Tuberville to give back to the brave men and women of the United States Armed Forces.

In 1989 Coach Tuberville met his future wife, Suzanne Fette, at the Sugar Bowl in New Orleans. They were married in 1991 and have two boys, Tucker and Troy, who continue to make their dad proud.

In his career as a College Football Coach, Tommy started a long and successful career at the University of Miami, starting as a graduate assistant and rising to the Hurricanes' defensive coordinator. He led the Canes to three AP national championships and coached future Hall of Famers Ray Lewis and Warren Sapp, as well as Dwayne "The Rock" Johnson.

In 1998, Coach Tuberville was announced as the next head coach for the Auburn Tigers. Tuberville’s time at Auburn was a success by any measure, as the Tigers captured eight consecutive bowl appearances, including five New Year’s Day bowl appearances, five SEC Western Division titles, one SEC Championship, and a 13-0 season in 2004, after which Coach Tuberville was once again named the AP’s SEC Coach of the Year. For his efforts during the perfect 2004 season, Tuberville would be named Paul “Bear” Bryant Coach of the Year, Associated Press Coach of the Year, Walter Camp Coach of the Year, Sporting News Coach of the Year, and AFCA Coach of the Year, among others.

_In 2020, Coach Tuberville became Senator Tuberville, winning his election to represent all Alabamians in the United States Senate with the full support of President Trump. Since then, he's supported President Trump's America-First policies while fighting off the Radical Left. In his first term as Senator, Tommy was one of eight Republicans to vote against certifying the 2020 election, has been a strong pro-life advocate, and called out Democrats for being pro-crime.

Now, Coach Tuberville is running for Governor so he can continue representing Alabamians and Americans in his homestate of Alabama. The Radical Left thinks they can flip Alabama blue and turn it into the California of the South. Can he count on your support? _

** [**HELP COACH TUBERVILLE**](http://links.tubervilleforalabama.com/u/click?_t=b8b4efbe79f64b579dfa7d8da685a7a9&_m=48e85192c204403fb9de4978132b10c8&_e=VXQbmJx5Lsy_POK2HbOpuoHRxaPlO5p4qydzmKViis-BKrhXiBsshBvfLjKaGKoE5rLdYLZfLFt76pOtPzi8nlOeLZLBTphoBTVQ7VDlCOFUOLYCGzhKe0-pWEQTMdI_gS-Nhnla8Ui09Q_-cMJec7-7ZCC5N2z-eQqnpgD5gMN0rZKmGYY3_ed6BRaTn_DWfh5FZyGDNKx4ZyXf-LQABBtj3NUPLnECGlV3xX-6GMRdwF7l6S3RKzEdp9RA_K5CoRNU-FcK1qcAN-U3_pCdOU_53FhmPAoeeucuzoq2ZtEas-m6m2-aCFncGGKXdfIiwCx4thM3dhX2vI7-Cv_6e6xF9YP0Z6qtMZRjoYGXrIIt0ofP1mrxwTVoL1qlIbUjveeq0XTN6izK9tBORnCg9QAXbMCGajLwCq6LBGYpeuCq856iomT81-LMH3KgjhA0w-WJYHWZCRqXx1E9DWeTvZj2RFWItRcL6XYAgzeJO9VHa_E1utDpLX6cCt_iJI7tm-r-PAhj8Vv6cxShq9z9CDFMfxo52z506iXZhcx47yRsm8MWcVJY0R5VLHH0PpG_)
